description: Geographic-diversity test locations for fajr's hilal classifier. These are NOT committee decisions; they are observation points chosen to span latitude (high-latitude polar to equatorial), elevation (sea level to high mountain), and Muslim-community context (major historical centers + diaspora communities). The dataset answers: does fajr's three-criterion classification produce sensible verdicts across the full geographic range users would encounter in production? Each location is run against the same Hijri month onsets in hilal-observations.json. Locations were chosen for: (a) active or historically-significant Muslim communities, (b) astronomical interest (extreme latitude or elevation), (c) under-represented in academic hilal literature relative to Saudi/Egypt/Pakistan-centric default. Combined output in docs/hilal-historical-analysis.md.

locations.name: Cape Town
locations.rationale: Southern tip of Africa, active Cape Malay Muslim community since the 1700s, Muslim Judicial Council (MJC) operates here. Important for testing Southern Hemisphere classification at moderate latitude.

locations.name: Sanaa
locations.rationale: Historical Muslim center at significant altitude. Active sighting tradition. Tests fajr at high elevation in a Muslim-majority context.

locations.name: Kabul
locations.rationale: Large Muslim community, mountainous setting. Tests at moderate altitude with regional sighting authority.

locations.name: Quito
locations.rationale: Equatorial AND high-elevation simultaneously — a regime not represented in standard hilal datasets. Small but active Muslim community.

locations.name: Lhasa
locations.rationale: Extreme high-elevation regime. Small Muslim presence (Hui community). Astronomically interesting because horizon dip + atmospheric pressure deviate strongly from sea-level baseline.

locations.name: Addis Ababa
locations.rationale: Significant Muslim minority (~30% of Ethiopia). Historical Muslim presence since 7th century. High-elevation East African regime.

locations.name: Tromsø
locations.rationale: Within the Arctic Circle. Small Muslim community follows Norwegian Council of Muftis guidance. For some Hijri months sun-Earth geometry makes naked-eye sighting astronomically impossible regardless of crescent age.

locations.name: Anchorage
locations.rationale: Sub-Arctic. Small US Muslim community. Tests Western-Hemisphere high-latitude classification.

locations.name: Reykjavik
locations.rationale: Small mosque, growing Muslim community. Atlantic-island high-latitude case.

locations.name: Lagos
locations.rationale: Largest Muslim population in West Africa. Nigerian Supreme Council for Islamic Affairs operates here. Sea-level equatorial counterpart to Quito.

locations.name: Tashkent
locations.rationale: Central Asia, historical Naqshbandi center, Muslim Spiritual Board of Uzbekistan operates here. Underrepresented in academic hilal literature dominated by Arab/South-Asian sources.

locations.name: Mauritius (Port Louis)
locations.rationale: Indian Ocean island, ~17% Muslim population, active Sunni and Shia communities. Geographically isolated case for cross-criterion comparison.

locations.name: Buenos Aires
locations.rationale: Southern-hemisphere Western-hemisphere case. Small Muslim community (mostly Syrian-Lebanese descent). Tests symmetry with Cape Town across continents.

locations.name: Dakar
locations.rationale: Tijaniyya Sufi tradition, ~95% Muslim Senegal. Strong local Maliki tradition, distinct from North African sighting traditions.

locations.name: Mecca
locations.rationale: The reference Muslim location. Included for direct comparison — fajr's prediction at Mecca itself for each Hijri month is the natural baseline.

locations.name: Madinah
locations.rationale: Second-most-significant Muslim site. Slightly elevated, slightly more northerly than Mecca.
